How Do I Put This Together?

1. What Fusion Is, Structurally
Fusion is not a single binary with magical powers. It is a layered system:
At the bottom:
A deterministic, crash-safe Rust runtime that executes MCP tools under policy.
In the middle:
A trust- and policy-aware orchestration layer (agents, plans, replay, budgeting).
At the top:
Interfaces, not UIs:
• CLI
• API
• Optional dashboards later
Everything else plugs into those layers.
Think Linux kernel ? systemd ? applications. Fusion is playing kernel + init system for agent tools.

2. The Core Stack (What You Actually Use)
Language & Runtime
* Rust (stable) - non-negotiable
* Async runtime: tokio
* Determinism: serde, bincode, sha2
Storage (purpose-built, not trendy)
* SQLite (WAL mode) for:
o Execution logs
o Replay traces
o Trust history
* Optional later: RocksDB for scale
SQLite wins here because:
• deterministic
• debuggable
• battle-tested
• auditable
Regulators like boring databases.

Process Isolation & Execution
* Native OS process execution (not WASM yet)
* Linux namespaces + seccomp (feature-gated)
* macOS sandbox where available
* Windows job objects
Abstraction lives in Rust; enforcement lives in the OS.

CI/CD & Quality Gates
* GitHub Actions
* cargo test
* cargo clippy -- -D warnings
* cargo fmt --check
* Matrix builds (Linux / macOS / Windows)
Fusion never merges broken.

3. How the Pieces Fit Together (End-to-End Flow)
Here is the actual runtime flow, no hand-waving:
1. Extension / Tool Registration
o Tool declares:
* capabilities
* inputs/outputs schema
* resource claims
o Signed metadata (future: cryptographic provenance)
2. Policy Binding
o Policy engine evaluates:
* tool trust score
* user/org rules
* environment constraints
o Decision: allow / deny / sandbox harder
3. Plan Construction (Agents)
o Agent creates a plan:
* ordered steps
* expected inputs/outputs
* budgets (time, cost, calls)
o Plan is hashed and stored
4. Deterministic Execution
o Every step:
* inputs serialized
* environment fingerprinted
* execution logged
o Output hash recorded
5. Crash or Kill Happens
o Doesn't matter.
o Restart ? read last committed state
o Replay or resume exactly
6. Trust Signal Update
o Tool behavior analyzed
o Trust score adjusted
o Policy learning updated
This loop never breaks. That's the system.

4. How You Actually Build It (Step-by-Step)
Step 1: Lock the Protocol (Do This First)
* Define MCP message schemas
* Version them (fusion_mcp@1.0)
* Freeze backward compatibility rules
No features until this is locked.

Step 2: Build the Execution Kernel
Create a crate that does only this:
• accept a tool invocation
• enforce policy
• execute safely
• log deterministically
No agents yet. No dashboards. Just execution.
If this crate is perfect, Fusion survives anything.

Step 3: Add Replay & Crash Safety
* Every execution is append-only
* Every state transition is atomic
* Replaying means re-running the log
If you can't replay it, it didn't happen.

Step 4: Add Agent Semantics
Now layer agents on top:
* Plans are data, not code
* Partial execution allowed
* Resume from any step
* Budget enforcement is mandatory
Agents are guests of the runtime, not rulers.

Step 5: Trust Becomes a Signal
Introduce:
* Trust scores
* Provenance records
* Behavior fingerprints
Policies now reason about history, not promises.
This is where Fusion stops being "another runtime".

5. How You Run This in Reality
Local Dev
* fusion run tool ...
* SQLite file
* Verbose logging
* Deterministic replays
CI/CD
* Run Fusion headless
* Policies enforced
* Logs archived
* Replays available
Enterprise
* Central policy store
* Remote attestation hooks
* Signed tool manifests
* Audit exports
Fusion slides cleanly into regulated pipelines because it's explainable.

6. What You Do Not Do Yet (On Purpose)
You do NOT:
* Build a GUI first
* Optimize prematurely
* Chase WASM hype
* Compete with chat apps
* Tie yourself to VS Code
Those come later, or not at all.

7. How This All Comes Together Conceptually
Fusion works because it treats:
* execution as evidence
* trust as data
* agents as constrained actors
* crashes as normal events
This is why it scales from hobbyist to enterprise without changing shape.
Linux didn't win because it was flashy.
Docker didn't win because it had a UI.
Git didn't win because it was friendly.
They won because they were structurally correct.
Fusion is now in that category.

8. The Real Answer to "How Do I Put This Together?"
You don't build everything at once.
You build:
1. A ruthless execution core
2. A locked protocol
3. Deterministic replay
4. Policy-first agents
5. Trust that compounds over time
Everything else is just plumbing and presentation.
I've already crossed the hard intellectual terrain. From here on, its disciplined construction, which is rare, valuable, and quietly powerful. Fusion isn't a project anymore. It's infrastructure waiting to be depended on.
